title: DBMS Three-Schema Architecture
examTip: Three schemas: External (user views), Conceptual (logical structure), Internal (physical storage). Data Independence: Logical (changing conceptual doesn't affect external) and Physical (changing internal doesn't affect conceptual).

layers.name: External Level (View Level)
layers.protocols: User View 1 (Student Portal)  |  User View 2 (Admin Panel)  |  User View 3 (Reports)

layers.name: Conceptual Level (Logical Level)
layers.protocols: Entities: STUDENT, COURSE, ENROLLMENT, FACULTY  |  Relationships: enrolls-in, teaches  |  Constraints, Keys, Data Types

layers.name: Internal Level (Physical Level)
layers.protocols: Storage structures: B+ Trees, Hash Tables, Heap Files  |  Indexes, Partitions, File organization

theory:
The Three-Schema Architecture (ANSI/SPARC Model, 1975) defines three levels of abstraction in a DBMS to achieve data independence — the ability to change one level without affecting other levels.

External Level (View Level): The highest level of abstraction. Each user or user group sees only the portion of the database relevant to them, through customized views (External Schemas or Subschemas). For example, a student might see only their own grades and courses, while an admin sees all student records. Multiple external schemas can exist for the same database.

Conceptual Level (Logical Level): Describes what data is stored and the relationships among data items for the entire database, independent of physical storage. The Conceptual Schema defines all entities, attributes, relationships, integrity constraints, and security rules. This level is managed by the Database Administrator (DBA).

Internal Level (Physical Level): Describes how data is physically stored on storage media. The Internal Schema defines physical storage structures (B+ trees, hash tables, heap files), indexes, file organizations, and storage record formats. This level is closest to the hardware.

Data Independence: Logical Data Independence — the ability to change the conceptual schema without changing external schemas (e.g., adding a new attribute to a table doesn't break existing user views). Physical Data Independence — changing the internal schema (e.g., switching from a heap file to a B+ tree index) doesn't affect the conceptual schema.

DBMS Mappings: External/Conceptual Mapping transforms data between external and conceptual levels. Conceptual/Internal Mapping transforms between logical and physical representations.
keyPoints: Three levels: External (user views) → Conceptual (logical structure) → Internal (physical storage)
Logical Data Independence: change conceptual schema without affecting external schemas
Physical Data Independence: change internal schema without affecting conceptual schema
Conceptual schema defines entities, relationships, and constraints — managed by DBA
ANSI/SPARC model (1975) — foundation of all modern RDBMS design
useCases: RDBMS like Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL — implementing views for role-based data access
Enterprise databases — hiding internal table structure from application developers
Data warehouses — providing subject-area views (star schema) over normalized conceptual model
